ladders.title: Full Problem — Strange Printer with Brute Force Recursion
ladders.desc: Complete solution exploring all possible print sequences recursively
ladders.explanation: The Strange Printer problem requires finding the minimum number of print operations needed to produce a target string, where each operation can print any character over any continuous segment, overwriting previous characters. The brute force approach explores all possible sequences by recursively considering each character as a starting point and merging identical adjacent characters. The intuition is that we can think of printing as covering the string with segments, where each segment prints a single character. By trying all possible ways to break the string into segments and merge identical adjacent segments, we explore all permutations of print operations. The algorithm works by recursively solving for substrings: for a substring s[i:j], we try all possible splits where we print s[i] from position i to some k, then recursively solve for the remaining parts. When s[i] equals s[k], we can merge these operations. The base case is when i >= j (empty substring), requiring 0 operations. This exhaustive search has factorial complexity because for each position, we try all possible segment endings, leading to O(n!) time in worst case. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth. Connection to L1: This uses the recursive helper function that computes minimum operations for substrings. Common mistakes include not handling empty substrings correctly, missing the merge optimization for identical characters, or having incorrect base cases. The real pattern involves memoization to avoid recomputation, though the brute force nature remains.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Minimum Operations for Substring
ladders.desc: Helper function that computes min operations for a substring using recursion
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core recursive function that calculates the minimum number of print operations required for a substring s[i:j] (from index i inclusive to j exclusive). The intuition is to break the problem into smaller subproblems by considering all possible ways to print the first character s[i] and then recursively solve the remaining parts. The key insight is that when printing s[i], we can extend the print segment to any position k (where i <= k < j), and if s[i] equals s[k], we can merge these operations, reducing the cost. The algorithm defines a function solve(i, j): if i >= j, return 0 (empty substring). Otherwise, initialize answer to a large value. For each k from i to j-1, compute cost = 1 + solve(i+1, k) + solve(k+1, j), but if s[i] == s[k], we can merge, so cost = solve(i, k) + solve(k+1, j) [since printing s[i] from i to k might be combined]. Actually, careful: standard approach is to try printing s[i] from i to k, then recursively solve for the rest. The merge optimization is handled by considering that if we print s[i] up to k, and s[i] == s[k], we might save operations. In brute force, we simply try all k and compute recursively. Time complexity for this sub-routine on a substring of length m is O(m!) in worst case due to recursive branching. Space complexity is O(m) for recursion stack. Connection to L0: This function is called repeatedly in the full solution to compute results for all substrings. Connection to L2: It relies on the core logic of trying all segment endings. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices, incorrect base case handling, or not considering all k values. Real code pattern uses memoization (e.g., @lru_cache) to store results for (i,j) pairs, though in pure brute force, it recomputes.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Trying All Segment Endings for a Character
ladders.desc: The technique of iterating over all possible end positions when printing a character
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves, for a given starting index i with character c = s[i], iterating through all possible end positions k (from i to n-1) to determine where to stop printing c in a continuous segment. The intuition is that when the printer prints character c, it can cover any contiguous block from i to k, and after that, we need to handle the remaining string. By trying all k, we exhaustively explore all ways to apply the first print operation. This is central to the brute force approach because it captures all possibilities for how the first operation might be applied, leading to recursive exploration of the remaining subproblems. The step-by-step process: given substring s[i:j], for each k in [i, j-1], consider printing c from i to k. Then, the cost is 1 (for this print) plus the cost to print s[i+1:k] (if any) and s[k+1:j] (the rest). However, if c equals s[k], we might merge operations, which in some formulations reduces cost. In brute force, we simply compute cost recursively for each k and take the minimum. Time complexity for this step on a substring of length m is O(m) per recursive call, but due to recursion, it leads to O(m!) overall. Space complexity is O(m) for recursion depth. Connection to L1: This logic is embedded in the recursive function as the loop over k. Connection to L3: It relies on understanding substrings and recursion. Common mistakes include not including k = i (printing only at position i) or k = j-1 (printing up to the end), or mishandling the merge condition. Real code pattern uses a for loop from i to j-1 and recursive calls.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Subproblem Decomposition
ladders.desc: Breaking the problem into smaller subproblems using recursion
ladders.explanation: This building block involves the concept of solving a problem by recursively breaking it into smaller instances of the same problem. For the Strange Printer, the subproblem is finding the minimum operations for a substring s[i:j]. The intuition is that if we can solve for smaller substrings, we can combine their solutions to solve for larger ones. This is a classic divide-and-conquer or dynamic programming technique, but here used in a brute force manner. The algorithm defines a function that, for a given substring, tries all possible first operations and recursively solves the remaining parts. The base case is when the substring is empty (i >= j), requiring 0 operations. The recursive case computes the answer by taking the minimum over all possible splits. Time complexity depends on the number of subproblems and how they are combined; in this brute force, it leads to exponential time. Space complexity is O(n) due to recursion depth. Connection to L2: This decomposition uses the core logic of trying segment endings to define the recursive steps. Connection to L4: It relies on basic string operations to define substrings. Common mistakes include not defining base cases correctly, infinite recursion due to incorrect bounds, or not covering all possible splits. Real code pattern uses if i >= j: return 0, then a loop and recursive calls.

In the Strange Printer algorithm, we frequently need to get the character at a starting position i (s[i]) to determine what to print, and compare it with characters at other positions (e.g., s[k]) to check if they are the same, which allows for merging print operations. The intuition is simple: strings are sequences of characters, and we can use indices to refer to specific positions. Accessing is typically O(1) time, and comparison is O(1) as well. These operations are foundational because the algorithm's logic revolves around examining and manipulating characters based on their values. Step-by-step: given a string s and indices i and k, we retrieve s[i] and s[k] using array indexing, then use the == operator to check if they are equal. approaches.name: Top-Down DP with Memoization O(n^3)
approaches.summary: Use recursion with memoization to solve subproblems where dp[i][j] represents the minimum turns to print substring s[i:j+1]. Break the problem by considering splits and matching characters to reduce redundant calculations. This avoids recomputation by storing results in a memo table.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Strange Printer with Top-Down DP Memoization
ladders.desc: Complete solution using recursion with memoization to find minimum turns to print any string.
ladders.explanation: The Strange Printer problem requires finding the minimum number of turns needed to print a given string, where each turn can print any number of the same character in consecutive positions, potentially overwriting previous prints. The top-down DP approach with memoization solves this by breaking the problem into overlapping subproblems. The key insight is that dp[i][j] represents the minimum turns to print substring s[i:j+1]. The recurrence relation considers two cases: 1) Print s[i] separately, then solve for the rest: 1 + dp[i+1][j]. 2) If there exists some k where s[i] == s[k], we can print s[i] and s[k] together in one turn, then solve for the middle and right parts separately: dp[i+1][k-1] + dp[k][j]. We take the minimum over all possible k. This yields O(n³) time due to three nested loops in the recursion, and O(n²) space for the memo table. The approach avoids recomputation by storing results, making it efficient for strings up to length 100. Common mistakes include not handling base cases (i > j returns 0), incorrect indexing, or missing the optimization when characters match. This method connects to L1 by relying on the recursive helper function to compute dp values.

Option 1: 1 + dp[1][2] = 1+2=3. Option 2: k=2: dp[1][1] + dp[2][2] = 0 + 1 = 1. So min=1, but that's incorrect because printing s[0] and s[2] together requires one turn, but then we need to print s[1]='b', which might be covered? Actually, after printing 'a' at positions 0 and 2, position 1 is not printed yet, so we need another turn for 'b'. So total turns = 1 (for 'a') + dp[1][1]? Wait, the recurrence dp[i][j] = min(1 + dp[i+1][j], min over k where s[i]==s[k] of dp[i+1][k-1] + dp[k][j]). For k=2: dp[1][1] + dp[2][2] = 0 + 1 = 1, but this only covers printing s[0] and s[2] as 'a', and assumes s[1] is handled separately? Actually, dp[2][2] covers substring "a" at index 2, which is just one character. But we need to include s[1] in the total. The recurrence might be flawed. Let's use standard approach: dp[i][j] = min(1 + dp[i+1][j], min over k where s[i]==s[k] of dp[i+1][k-1] + dp[k][j]). For "aba", i=0,j=2. Option 1: 1 + dp[1][2] = 1+2=3. Option 2: k=2: dp[1][1] + dp[2][2] = 0 + 1 = 1. So min=1, but that's wrong. Correct recurrence: dp[i][j] = min(1 + dp[i+1][j], min over k where s[i]==s[k] of dp[i][k-1] + dp[k+1][j])? Actually, standard solution: dp[i][j] = min(dp[i][j], dp[i][k-1] + dp[k][j]) for k where s[i]==s[k]. Let's use that. For "aba", dp[0][2]. Initialize dp[0][2] = 1 + dp[1][2]. dp[1][2]=2 as before. So dp[0][2]=3. Then check k=2: s[0]==s[2], so dp[0][1] + dp[2][2]. Compute dp[0][1] for "ab": s[0]='a', dp[0][1] = 1 + dp[1][1] = 1+1=2. So dp[0][2] = min(3, 2+1) = 3. Still not 2. Hmm, let's think: The printer can print any number of same character in one turn. For "aba", we can print 'a' at positions 0 and 2 in one turn, then print 'b' at position 1 in another turn, so 2 turns. The DP should capture this. Let's use correct algorithm: function solve(i,j): if i>j return 0. if memo[i][j] set, return it. ans = 1 + solve(i+1,j). for k in range(i+1, j+1): if s[i]==s[k]: ans = min(ans, solve(i, k-1) + solve(k+1, j)). memo[i][j]=ans. For "aba", i=0,j=2. ans = 1 + solve(1,2). solve(1,2): i=1,j=2. ans = 1 + solve(2,2). solve(2,2)=1 (base case). So solve(1,2)=2. So initial ans=3. Then k=2: s[0]==s[2], so ans = min(3, solve(0,1) + solve(3,2)). solve(0,1): i=0,j=1. ans = 1 + solve(1,1). solve(1,1)=1. So solve(0,1)=2. solve(3,2): i=3>j, return 0. So ans = min(3, 2+0) = 2. ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Helper Function with Memoization
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that computes dp[i][j] for substrings.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the top-down DP approach. It defines a function solve(i, j) that returns the minimum turns to print substring s[i:j+1]. The intuition is to break the problem into smaller subproblems by considering two scenarios: either print the first character s[i] separately, or group it with another matching character later in the substring. The function uses memoization to store results in a 2D array memo, avoiding exponential recomputation. The algorithm: Base case: if i > j, return 0 (empty substring). If memo[i][j] is already computed, return it. Otherwise, initialize ans = 1 + solve(i+1, j) (printing s[i] alone). Then, iterate k from i+1 to j: if s[i] == s[k], update ans = min(ans, solve(i, k-1) + solve(k+1, j)). This considers printing s[i] and s[k] together in one turn, then solving the left and right parts separately. Finally, store ans in memo[i][j] and return it. Time complexity for this function on a substring of length m is O(m²) in the worst case due to the loop over k, but overall across all calls, it aggregates to O(n³). Space complexity is O(n²) for the memo table. This sub-routine connects to L0 by being called recursively to solve the entire problem. Common mistakes include incorrect base cases, off-by-one errors in indices, or not checking memo before computation. The real code pattern involves a nested function with @lru_cache or explicit memo dictionary.

examples.trace: Call solve(0,1). i=0,j=1. Check memo[0][1] not set. ans = 1 + solve(1,1). Call solve(1,1): i=1,j=1. Check memo[1][1] not set. ans = 1 + solve(2,1). i=2>j=1, so solve(2,1) returns 0. So ans=1. Store memo[1][1]=1, return 1. Back to solve(0,1): ans = 1 + 1 = 2. Now loop k from i+1=1 to j=1: k=1, s[0]='a', s[1]='b', not equal, so no update. Store memo[0][1]=2, return 2.

Call solve(0,1). i=0,j=1. ans = 1 + solve(1,1). solve(1,1) returns 1 (as above). So ans=2. Loop k from 1 to 1: k=1, s[0]=='a', s[1]=='a', equal. Update ans = min(2, solve(0,0) + solve(2,1)). solve(0,0): i=0,j=0. ans = 1 + solve(1,0). i=1>j=0, solve(1,0) returns 0. So ans=1. Store memo[0][0]=1, return 1. solve(2,1): i=2>j=1, return 0. So ans = min(2, 1+0) = 1. Store memo[0][1]=1, return 1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP Recurrence Relation for Minimum Turns
ladders.desc: Derive and apply the recurrence relation that defines dp[i][j].
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the Strange Printer problem is the recurrence relation that computes the minimum turns for any substring. The relation is: dp[i][j] = min(1 + dp[i+1][j], min over k where i < k ≤ j and s[i] == s[k] of dp[i][k-1] + dp[k+1][j]). Intuition: For substring s[i:j+1], we have two choices. First, print s[i] separately in one turn, then solve for the rest s[i+1:j+1], giving 1 + dp[i+1][j]. Second, if there is another character s[k] equal to s[i], we can print s[i] and s[k] together in the same turn. However, after printing them together, the substring between i and k-1 might need separate handling, and the substring after k might also need turns. Thus, we add dp[i][k-1] (for the left part) and dp[k+1][j] (for the right part). Note that when k = i+1, dp[i][k-1] = dp[i][i], which is 1. The recurrence minimizes over all such k. This relation works because it considers all possible ways to group the first character with others, leveraging optimal substructure. Time analysis: Evaluating this relation for all i,j requires O(n³) due to nested loops. Space is O(n²) to store dp. Connection to L1: This relation is implemented in the recursive helper function. Common mistakes include incorrect indices, especially when k = j leading to dp[k+1][j] being out of bounds (should return 0), or not handling empty substrings properly. The real code pattern uses loops and conditional checks.

examples.trace: Compute dp[0][1]. Option 1: 1 + dp[1][1]. dp[1][1] = 1 (since single character). So option 1 = 2. Option 2: Find k where s[0]==s[k] and 0<k≤1. k=1: s[0]='a', s[1]='b', not equal. So no option 2. Thus dp[0][1] = min(2) = 2.

Compute dp[0][1]. Option 1: 1 + dp[1][1] = 1+1=2. Option 2: k=1: s[0]=='a', s[1]=='a', equal. Then dp[0][0] + dp[2][1]. dp[0][0] = 1 (single char). dp[2][1]: since 2>1, it's empty substring, value 0. So option 2 = 1+0=1. Thus dp[0][1] = min(2,1) = 1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Memoization for Overlapping Subproblems
ladders.desc: Implement memoization to store and reuse results of subproblems.
ladders.explanation: Memoization is a technique to optimize recursive algorithms by storing the results of expensive function calls and returning the cached result when the same inputs occur again. In the Strange Printer problem, subproblems dp[i][j] are computed multiple times due to the recursive nature, leading to exponential time without memoization. The intuition is to use a 2D array or dictionary to cache results. When solve(i,j) is called, first check if memo[i][j] is already computed; if yes, return it immediately. Otherwise, compute the result, store it in memo[i][j], and then return it. This reduces time complexity from exponential to O(n³). The memo table has size n×n, where n is the string length, giving O(n²) space. Connection to L2: This building block is essential to implement the DP recurrence efficiently. Common mistakes include using incorrect data structures (e.g., list of lists without initialization), not handling out-of-bounds indices, or forgetting to store results before returning. Real code pattern: Initialize memo as a 2D list with None, or use @lru_cache decorator in Python.

examples.trace: Initialize memo as 3x3 list (for n=2, indices 0..2) with None. Call solve(0,1). Check memo[0][1] is None, so compute. During computation, call solve(1,1). Check memo[1][1] is None, compute solve(1,1): returns 1, store memo[1][1]=1. Back to solve(0,1): compute result 2, store memo[0][1]=2. Final memo: memo[1][1]=1, memo[0][1]=2, others None.

Initialize memo 3x3 with None. Call solve(0,1). memo[0][1] is None, compute. Calls solve(1,1): memo[1][1] None, compute returns 1, store memo[1][1]=1. Then in solve(0,1), calls solve(0,0): memo[0][0] None, compute returns 1, store memo[0][0]=1. Compute result 1, store memo[0][1]=1. Final memo: memo[0][0]=1, memo[0][1]=1, memo[1][1]=1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Over Substrings and Matching Characters
ladders.desc: Loop through indices to find matching characters and split substrings.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves iterating over indices i and j to define substrings, and within that, looping k to find characters that match s[i]. The intuition is to explore all possible ways to group the first character of a substring with later occurrences. For a given substring s[i:j+1], we set i as the start index and j as the end index. Then, we iterate k from i+1 to j to check if s[k] equals s[i]. If a match is found, it indicates that s[i] and s[k] can be printed in the same turn, so we consider splitting the substring into two parts: s[i:k-1] and s[k+1:j]. This operation is fundamental to building the DP recurrence. Time complexity for a fixed i,j is O(j-i), which in the full algorithm contributes to O(n³). Space is O(1) for the loops. Connection to L3: This operation is used within the memoized function to compute dp[i][j]. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop ranges (e.g., including k=i or missing k=j), or not handling cases where k+1 > j. Real code pattern: for k in range(i+1, j+1): if s[i] == s[k]: ...

examples.trace: String s="aba", i=0, j=2. s[0]='a'. Loop k from 1 to 2: k=1, s[1]='b', not equal. k=2, s[2]='a', equal. So matching k=2.
s="aab", i=0, j=2. s[0]='a'. Loop k from 1 to 2: k=1, s[1]='a', equal. k=2, s[2]='b', not equal. So matching k=1.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Substrings and Recursive Calls
ladders.desc: Learn to define and manipulate substrings using indices in recursive functions.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept involves understanding how to represent substrings of a string using start and end indices, and how to make recursive calls on smaller substrings. A substring s[i:j+1] includes characters from index i to j inclusive. In recursion, we break a problem into smaller subproblems by reducing the index range. For example, to solve for s[i:j+1], we might call the function on s[i+1:j+1] or s[i:k-1] and s[k+1:j]. The intuition is that solving a larger problem depends on solutions to its parts. This is a key principle in divide-and-conquer and dynamic programming. Time and space analysis depends on the number of recursive calls, but here we focus on the concept. Connection to L4: This foundation is used when iterating over k to split substrings. Common mistakes include incorrect index arithmetic (e.g., using j instead of j+1), or not handling empty substrings (when i > j). Real code pattern: Define a function f(i,j) that calls f(i+1,j) or f(i,k-1) and f(k+1,j).

examples.trace: For string "ab" of length 2, to solve for entire string (i=0,j=1), we may need to solve for substring "b" (i=1,j=1) and possibly empty substrings. Recursive calls: f(0,1) calls f(1,1). f(1,1) is a base case (single character).

String "abc", indices 0,1,2. If we split at k=1, we get two substrings: s[0:0] (i=0, k-1=0) which is "a", and s[2:2] (k+1=2, j=2) which is "c". But note: s[0:0] means character at index 0, so it's "a". Actually, s[i:k-1] for i=0,k=1 gives s[0:0], which is substring from 0 to 0 inclusive, so it's "a". s[k+1:j] for k=1,j=2 gives s[2:2], which is "c".

approaches.name: Bottom-Up DP Iteration O(n^3)
approaches.summary: Iteratively compute dp[i][j] for all substrings in increasing length order, using transitions that account for printing characters in segments and handling identical endpoints. This builds the solution from the smallest substrings to the entire string.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Strange Printer with Bottom-Up DP O(n³)
ladders.desc: Complete solution computing minimum turns to print any string using bottom-up DP with O(n³) time and O(n²) space.
ladders.explanation: The Strange Printer problem requires finding the minimum number of turns needed to print a given string, where each turn can print any number of identical characters in a contiguous segment, but the printer can overwrite characters. The key insight is that we can model this as a dynamic programming problem on substrings. Define dp[i][j] as the minimum turns to print substring s[i:j+1]. The base case is dp[i][i] = 1 for single characters. For longer substrings, we consider two cases: 1) If s[i] == s[j], we can potentially print them together in one operation, so dp[i][j] = dp[i][j-1] (since printing s[i] covers s[j] too). 2) Otherwise, we split the substring at every possible k (i ≤ k < j) and combine results: dp[i][j] = min(dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] for all k). However, this naive split doesn't account for cases where endpoints match but internal characters differ. The correct recurrence is: dp[i][j] = min(dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] for all k where s[k] == s[i]). This ensures we consider splits where the first part ends with a character matching s[i], allowing efficient merging. We compute dp in increasing length order from length 1 to n. Intuition: By building from smallest substrings, we ensure subproblems are solved before larger ones. The O(n³) time comes from three nested loops: length, start index, and split point. Space is O(n²) for the DP table. Connection to L1: This uses the key subroutine of computing dp for a given substring using the recurrence. Common mistakes include incorrect base cases, missing the condition s[k] == s[i] in splits, or off-by-one errors in indices. Real code pattern: Initialize dp with large values, set diagonals to 1, iterate lengths, compute dp[i][j] using the recurrence, return dp[0][n-1].

examples.trace: String: "aba", n=3. Initialize dp 3x3 with INF, set dp[i][i]=1. Length 1: dp[0][0]=1, dp[1][1]=1, dp[2][2]=1. Length 2: dp[0][1]: s[0]='a', s[1]='b', different, split at k=0: dp[0][0]+dp[1][1]=1+1=2, so dp[0][1]=2. dp[1][2]: s[1]='b', s[2]='a', different, split at k=1: dp[1][1]+dp[2][2]=1+1=2, so dp[1][2]=2. Length 3: dp[0][2]: s[0]='a', s[2]='a', same so dp[0][2]=dp[0][1]=2. Final answer: dp[0][2]=2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Computing DP for a Single Substring
ladders.desc: Compute dp[i][j] for a fixed substring using the recurrence relation with splits.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on calculating the minimum turns dp[i][j] for a specific substring s[i:j+1], given that all smaller subproblems (shorter lengths) have already been computed. The recurrence is: dp[i][j] = 1 if i == j (base case). Otherwise, if s[i] == s[j], we set dp[i][j] = dp[i][j-1] because we can print s[i] in one operation that also covers s[j] if they match. If s[i] != s[j], we need to consider splits: dp[i][j] = min(dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] for all k where i ≤ k < j and s[k] == s[i]). The intuition is that we split the substring into two parts at index k, but only when s[k] matches s[i] to allow efficient merging of operations. This ensures we account for cases where the first character can be printed in segments. The algorithm: Initialize result to a large value. If i == j, return 1. If s[i] == s[j], set result = dp[i][j-1]. Then, for k from i to j-1, if s[k] == s[i], compute candidate = dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] and update result = min(result, candidate). Return result. Time complexity for this subroutine is O(n) in the worst case (iterating over k). Space is O(1) plus the DP table. Connection to L0: This is the core computation used in the nested loops of the full solution. Common mistakes include forgetting the condition s[k] == s[i], incorrect indexing in dp[k+1][j], or not handling the s[i] == s[j] case properly. Real code pattern: Function compute_dp(i, j, s, dp): if i == j: return 1; if s[i] == s[j]: ans = dp[i][j-1]; else: ans = INF; for k in range(i, j): if s[k] == s[i]: ans = min(ans, dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j]); return ans.

examples.trace: Given s="aba", i=0, j=2. dp[0][0]=1, dp[0][1]=2, dp[1][1]=1, dp[1][2]=2, dp[2][2]=1. Check s[i]='a', s[j]='a', they are equal, so set ans = dp[0][1] = 2. Then iterate k from 0 to 1: k=0: s[0]='a' matches s[i], candidate = dp[0][0] + dp[1][2] = 1 + 2 = 3, ans = min(2,3)=2. k=1: s[1]='b' does not match s[i], skip. Final ans=2.

Given s="abac", i=1, j=3. dp[1][1]=1, dp[1][2]=2, dp[2][2]=1, dp[2][3]=? (unknown, but assume computed as 2 for trace), dp[3][3]=1. Check s[i]='b', s[j]='c', different, so ans = INF. Iterate k from 1 to 2: k=1: s[1]='b' matches s[i], candidate = dp[1][1] + dp[2][3] = 1 + 2 = 3, ans = 3. k=2: s[2]='a' does not match s[i], skip. Final ans=3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recurrence Relation for Substring DP
ladders.desc: Derive and apply the recurrence dp[i][j] = min(dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j]) with endpoint matching condition.
ladders.explanation: The core logic is the recurrence relation that defines how to compute dp[i][j] based on smaller subproblems. The recurrence is: dp[i][j] = 1 if i == j. If s[i] == s[j], then dp[i][j] = dp[i][j-1] because printing s[i] can cover s[j] in the same operation if they are identical. Otherwise, dp[i][j] = min(dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] for all k where i ≤ k < j and s[k] == s[i]). The intuition behind this is dynamic programming on substrings: we want to find the optimal way to print s[i:j+1] by considering splits into two parts. The condition s[k] == s[i] is crucial because it allows the first part to end with the same character as the start, enabling us to merge operations efficiently. Without this condition, we might overcount turns. For example, in string "aba", splitting at k=1 without the condition would give dp[0][1]+dp[2][2]=2+1=3, but the correct answer is 2 because we can print 'a' in one operation covering both ends. The recurrence ensures we only consider splits where the split point character matches the start character, which aligns with the printer's ability to print contiguous segments of the same character. Step-by-step: First, handle base case. Then, if endpoints match, use dp[i][j-1]. Else, iterate over k, check if s[k] matches s[i], compute sum, take minimum. Time analysis for computing one dp[i][j] is O(n) due to the loop over k. Space is O(1). Connection to L1: This recurrence is implemented in the key subroutine. Common mistakes include using s[k] == s[j] instead of s[i], or missing the endpoint matching case. Real code pattern: ans = INF; if i == j: return 1; if s[i] == s[j]: ans = dp[i][j-1]; for k in range(i, j): if s[k] == s[i]: ans = min(ans, dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j]); return ans.

examples.trace: s[0]='a', s[1]='b', different. ans = INF. Iterate k from 0 to 0: k=0, s[0]='a' matches s[i]='a', candidate = dp[0][0] + dp[1][1] = 1 + 1 = 2. ans = 2. Return 2.
s[0]='a', s[1]='a', same. ans = dp[0][0] = 1. Return 1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Dynamic Programming on Substrings
ladders.desc: Solve a simpler DP problem: minimum operations to partition a string into palindromic substrings.
ladders.explanation: This building block introduces dynamic programming on substrings by solving a related but simpler problem: given a string, find the minimum number of partitions such that each substring is a palindrome. This helps understand how to define dp[i][j] and compute it iteratively. Define dp[i] as the minimum partitions needed for substring s[0:i+1], but for consistency with the main problem, we'll use dp[i][j] as the minimum partitions for s[i:j+1]. The recurrence: dp[i][j] = 0 if s[i:j+1] is a palindrome (no partition needed). Otherwise, dp[i][j] = min(dp[i][k] + 1 + dp[k+1][j] for all k where i ≤ k < j). The intuition is to split the substring into two parts at each possible k, add 1 for the partition at k, and sum the results. We compute dp in increasing length order. This teaches how to break a problem into subproblems and use a DP table. Step-by-step algorithm: Initialize dp with large values. For length from 1 to n, for i from 0 to n-length, set j = i+length-1. Check if s[i:j+1] is palindrome, if yes, dp[i][j]=0. Else, for k from i to j-1, compute candidate = dp[i][k] + 1 + dp[k+1][j], update dp[i][j] = min(dp[i][j], candidate). Time O(n³) for the loops, space O(n²). Connection to L2: This builds the skill of defining dp on substrings and using splits, which is directly used in the Strange Printer recurrence. Common mistakes include incorrect palindrome checking or off-by-one in indices. Real code pattern: n = len(s); dp = [[INF]*n for _ in range(n)]; for i in range(n): dp[i][i] = 0; for length in range(2, n+1): for i in range(n-length+1): j = i+length-1; if is_palindrome(s,i,j): dp[i][j]=0; else: for k in range(i, j): dp[i][j] = min(dp[i][j], dp[i][k] + 1 + dp[k+1][j]); return dp[0][n-1].

examples.trace: String "ab", n=2. Initialize dp 2x2 with INF. Length 1: dp[0][0]=0, dp[1][1]=0. Length 2: i=0, j=1. Check palindrome: "ab" is not palindrome. Iterate k from 0 to 0: k=0, candidate = dp[0][0] + 1 + dp[1][1] = 0 + 1 + 0 = 1. dp[0][1]=1. Return dp[0][1]=1.
String "aa", n=2. Initialize dp. Length 1: dp[0][0]=0, dp[1][1]=0. Length 2: i=0, j=1. Check palindrome: "aa" is palindrome, so dp[0][1]=0. Return 0.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Checking if a Substring is a Palindrome
ladders.desc: Determine if a given substring s[i:j+1] is a palindrome efficiently.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves checking whether a substring s[i:j+1] reads the same forwards and backwards. A palindrome is a string that is symmetric around its center. The straightforward way is to iterate from both ends: compare s[i] with s[j], s[i+1] with s[j-1], and so on, until the middle. If all pairs match, it's a palindrome; otherwise, it's not. For efficiency in DP contexts, we can precompute a palindrome table using dynamic programming: is_pal[i][j] is true if s[i:j+1] is palindrome. The recurrence: is_pal[i][j] = (s[i] == s[j]) and (j-i <= 1 or is_pal[i+1][j-1]). This means a substring is palindrome if its endpoints match and the inner substring (if length > 2) is also palindrome. We can compute this in O(n²) time by iterating lengths. Intuition: Building from smaller substrings to larger ones ensures we reuse results. Step-by-step algorithm for direct check: Initialize left = i, right = j. While left < right, if s[left] != s[right], return false; else, increment left, decrement right. If loop completes, return true. Time O(n) for each check, space O(1). For precomputation: Initialize is_pal with false. Set is_pal[i][i] = true for all i. For length from 2 to n, for i from 0 to n-length, j = i+length-1, set is_pal[i][j] = (s[i] == s[j]) and (length == 2 or is_pal[i+1][j-1]). Connection to L3: This operation is used in the palindrome partitioning problem to determine if a substring needs partitioning. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices or incorrect handling of empty substrings. Real code pattern: Function is_palindrome(s, i, j): while i < j: if s[i] != s[j]: return False; i+=1; j-=1; return True.

examples.trace: Substring s[0:7] = "racecar". left=0, right=6: s[0]='r', s[6]='r', match. left=1, right=5: s[1]='a', s[5]='a', match. left=2, right=4: s[2]='c', s[4]='c', match. left=3, right=3: loop ends. Return true.
Substring s[1:4] = "ell". left=1, right=3: s[1]='e', s[3]='l', mismatch, return false.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Substrings and Indices
ladders.desc: Learn how to represent and manipulate substrings using start and end indices in a string.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers the basics of working with substrings in programming. A substring is a contiguous sequence of characters within a string. In most programming languages, strings are indexed starting from 0. For a string s of length n, valid indices are 0 to n-1. A substring from index i to index j inclusive is denoted as s[i:j+1] in Python-like syntax. It includes characters from position i up to and including position j. If i > j, the substring is empty. Key operations: accessing a character at index i (s[i]), getting the length of substring (j-i+1), and iterating over characters from i to j. Intuition: Understanding indices is crucial for dynamic programming where we define dp[i][j] for substrings. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors, such as using s[i:j] which excludes j, or confusing inclusive vs exclusive ranges. Step-by-step: Given s = "hello", n=5. Indices: 0='h', 1='e', 2='l', 3='l', 4='o'. Substring from i=1 to j=3: s[1:4] = "ell" (length 3). To iterate, use a loop for idx in range(i, j+1): access s[idx]. Time for accessing is O(1) per character, space O(1) for indices. Connection to L4: This is used in palindrome checking to compare characters at specific indices. Real code pattern: s = "example"; i = 1; j = 3; substring = s[i:j+1] # gets "xam"; for k in range(i, j+1): print(s[k]).

examples.trace: String "algorithm", indices: 0='a',1='l',2='g',3='o',4='r',5='i',6='t',7='h',8='m'. Substring from i=2 to j=5 inclusive: characters at indices 2,3,4,5: 'g','o','r','i'. So substring = "gori", length = j-i+1 = 5-2+1 = 4.
String "data", indices: 0='d',1='a',2='t',3='a'. Substring from i=0 to j=2: indices 0,1,2: 'd','a','t'. Substring = "dat", length = 2-0+1 = 3.

approaches.name: DP with Greedy Optimization O(n^3)
approaches.summary: Enhance the bottom-up DP by greedily handling cases where endpoints match, reducing the number of transitions considered. This optimization leverages the property that printing identical characters together can minimize turns, slightly improving constant factors.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Strange Printer with DP and Greedy Optimization
ladders.desc: Complete solution using bottom-up DP with greedy optimization for identical endpoints
ladders.explanation: The Strange Printer problem requires finding the minimum number of turns to print a string where the printer can print any sequence of identical characters in one turn, but can only print over existing characters (not erase). The key insight is that we can model this as an interval DP problem where dp[i][j] represents the minimum turns to print substring s[i:j+1]. The greedy optimization comes from observing that when s[i] == s[j], we can potentially print them together in one operation, reducing the number of transitions we need to consider. The intuition: If the endpoints match, we can print that character first across the entire interval, then fill in the rest. This reduces the DP transitions from O(n^2) per state to O(n) in many cases. The algorithm builds dp table bottom-up: For each interval length from 1 to n, for each starting index i, compute dp[i][j] by considering all possible splits k where i ≤ k < j. When s[i] == s[j], we can set dp[i][j] = dp[i][j-1] because printing s[i] at the beginning covers s[j] too. Otherwise, we try all splits: dp[i][j] = min(dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j]) for all k. Time complexity is O(n³) due to three nested loops over intervals and splits, but the greedy optimization reduces constant factors. Space is O(n²) for the DP table. Connection to L1: This uses the optimized interval DP recurrence as its core engine. Common mistakes include not handling empty strings correctly, off-by-one errors in interval indices, and missing the greedy optimization case. Real code pattern: Initialize dp[n][n] with dp[i][i] = 1 for all i. For length from 2 to n, for i from 0 to n-length, j = i+length-1. If s[i] == s[j], dp[i][j] = dp[i][j-1]. Else dp[i][j] = min(dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j]) for k in [i, j-1]. Return dp[0][n-1].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Optimized Interval DP Recurrence
ladders.desc: The DP recurrence relation with greedy optimization for matching endpoints
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the core DP recurrence for the Strange Printer problem. Given a string s and indices i,j representing a substring, it computes the minimum turns needed to print s[i:j+1]. The recurrence has two cases based on whether the endpoints match. Intuition: When s[i] == s[j], we can print character s[i] in one turn that covers both endpoints, then fill the middle. This means the cost equals printing s[i:j] (excluding the last character) because s[j] gets printed for free with s[i]. When endpoints differ, we must split the interval at some point k and combine costs of two subproblems. This works because any optimal printing sequence will have some point where we change colors, and that split point defines two independent subproblems. The algorithm: If i > j, return 0 (empty string). If i == j, return 1 (single character). If s[i] == s[j], return dp(i, j-1). Else, return min over k from i to j-1 of dp(i,k) + dp(k+1,j). Time complexity for computing one state is O(n) in worst case due to the min over splits, but O(1) when endpoints match. Space is O(n²) for memoization. Connection to L0: This recurrence is called repeatedly in the full DP solution. Connection to L2: This builds on the interval splitting concept. Common mistakes: Forgetting the empty string base case, incorrect index bounds in the min loop, or not handling the matching endpoints case properly. Real code pattern: Use memoization with @lru_cache. Function dp(i,j): if i > j: return 0; if i == j: return 1; if s[i] == s[j]: return dp(i, j-1); ans = INF; for k in range(i, j): ans = min(ans, dp(i,k) + dp(k+1,j)); return ans.

examples.trace: Call dp(0,2). s[0]=='a', s[2]=='a', so endpoints match. Return dp(0,1). Call dp(0,1). s[0]!=s[1], so try splits: k=0: dp(0,0)+dp(1,1)=1+1=2. Return 2. So dp(0,2)=2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Interval Splitting for Independent Subproblems
ladders.desc: Breaking the problem into smaller intervals that can be solved independently
ladders.explanation: The core technique is dividing the string into two contiguous substrings at a split point k, solving each independently, and combining results. Intuition: In any optimal printing sequence, there will be moments where the printer changes color. Consider the first time it changes from color c1 to c2. All characters printed before this change form one contiguous block, and those after form another. This corresponds to splitting the string at some index k. The key insight is that the two subproblems are independent because once you print a character, it's fixed and won't be reprinted (except when overprinted with same color). Therefore, the minimum turns for interval [i,j] equals the minimum over all possible split points k of (turns for [i,k] + turns for [k+1,j]). This is a standard interval DP pattern. The algorithm: For a given interval, iterate k from i to j-1, compute cost for left and right parts, take the minimum sum. This reduces a complex sequence planning problem to simpler subproblems. Time complexity for one interval is O(n) due to iterating over k. Space is O(1) plus recursion/memoization overhead. Connection to L1: This splitting logic is used in the recurrence when endpoints don't match. Connection to L3: This relies on being able to compute turns for subintervals. Common mistakes: Splitting at k such that subproblems overlap (must be i ≤ k < j), or not considering all possible splits. Real code pattern: ans = INF; for k in range(i, j): ans = min(ans, cost(i,k) + cost(k+1,j)).

examples.trace: Interval [0,1] means substring "ab". Possible split points: k=0 only. Left: [0,0]="a" cost=1. Right: [1,1]="b" cost=1. Total=2. So min=2.

Interval [0,2]="aba". Splits: k=0: left [0,0] cost=1, right [1,2]="ba" cost=2 (compute: splits for "ba": k=1: left "b"=1, right "a"=1, total=2), so total=3. k=1: left [0,1]="ab" cost=2, right [2,2]="a" cost=1, total=3. min=3? Wait, but we know answer is 2 due to endpoints matching optimization. This example shows splitting alone gives 3, but with greedy optimization we get 2. So for this level, we're just demonstrating the splitting logic without optimization.

ladders.title: Building Block — Computing Minimum Turns for a Uniform String
ladders.desc: Solving the base case where all characters in the string are identical
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept handles the simplest non-trivial case: a string composed entirely of the same character (e.g., "aaaa"). Intuition: Since the printer can print any sequence of identical characters in one turn, and all characters are the same, the entire string can be printed in one turn. The printer starts with an empty tape, prints the character once across the entire length, and it's done. No need for multiple turns or overprinting. The algorithm is trivial: For any interval where all characters are identical, return 1. In practice, we can check by comparing s[i] to s[j] and ensuring all between are same, but for this building block, we assume it's given as uniform. Time complexity O(1) if we know it's uniform, or O(n) to verify. Space O(1). Connection to L2: This is a base case in the interval splitting—when an interval is uniform, no split is needed. Connection to L4: This uses the operation of printing a sequence of identical characters. Common mistakes: Overcomplicating by thinking multiple turns are needed, or not handling empty string (0 turns). Real code pattern: if string is empty: return 0; else if all chars same: return 1.

examples.trace: All characters are 'a'. Printer prints 'a' once across all positions. Done in 1 turn.
All characters are 'b'. One turn to print 'b' everywhere.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Printing a Sequence of Identical Characters
ladders.desc: The fundamental action of the printer: printing one or more of the same character in a single turn
ladders.explanation: This is the basic operation of the Strange Printer: in one turn, it can print a sequence of identical characters onto the tape. The tape is initially empty. The printer chooses a character c and a set of contiguous positions (could be non-contiguous in general, but for this problem, we consider it prints across a range), and prints c on those positions. If a position already has a character, it gets overprinted only if it's the same character; otherwise, the printer cannot change it to a different character (so planning order matters). Intuition: This operation is atomic—it's the smallest unit of work. The cost of printing is 1 turn regardless of how many positions are printed, as long as they're all the same character. The algorithm: To print a string, we sequence these operations. For example, to print "ab", we need two turns: one for 'a', one for 'b'. To print "aa", one turn suffices. Time complexity for one operation is O(1) conceptually. Space O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used to print uniform strings. Connection to L5: This relies on understanding what a turn means. Common mistakes: Thinking printing each character individually always takes one turn (it does, but we can batch same characters), or misunderstanding that overprinting with same character is allowed. Real code pattern: turn_count = 0; for each planned print operation: turn_count += 1.

examples.trace: Tape: empty. Turn 1: print 'a' at position 0. Tape: "a". Done. Total turns: 1.
Tape: empty. Turn 1: print 'a' at positions 0 and 1. Tape: "aa". Done. Total turns: 1.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Turns and Character Printing
ladders.desc: The basic idea of a printing turn and what it means to print a character
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept for beginners. A 'turn' is a single operation where the printer can print one or more characters onto a tape. The tape starts empty. In each turn, the printer chooses a character (like 'a', 'b', etc.) and prints it on some positions of the tape. Key points: 1) All characters printed in one turn must be the same. 2) Once a character is printed on a position, it can only be changed if the printer prints the same character again on top (overprinting). 3) The goal is to make the tape match a target string using as few turns as possible. Intuition: Think of it like painting a wall: you can paint a section with one color in one stroke, but to change colors, you need a new stroke. The challenge is planning the order of strokes to minimize total strokes. The algorithm at this level is just counting: if you print one character, that's one turn; if you print multiple of the same character together, that's still one turn. Time and space are not analyzed here—it's conceptual. Connection to L4: This defines what a printing operation is. Common mistakes: Confusing turns with characters printed, or thinking you can print different characters in one turn. Real code pattern: No code yet—just understanding that turns are counted, not characters.

examples.trace: Start: tape is empty. Turn 1: print 'a'. Now tape is "a". Done. Total turns: 1.
Start: empty. Turn 1: print 'a'. Tape: "a". Turn 2: print 'b'. Tape: "ab". Total turns: 2.
